title: Developing and Deploying a Platform that the Business Understands and Developers Actually Want
gist: At KubeCon & CloudNativeCon Europe, Lucas Hornung and Christian Matthaei described how their platform team survived a leadership crisis and drove company-wide adoption by treating internal-platform adoption as a persuasion and communication problem -- using DORA metrics to get a business hearing and narrative/personas to win developer buy-in -- rather than a purely technical one.

themes.theme: Platform adoption as a persuasion problem

insights.claim: Hornung and Matthaei concluded that technical adoption of an internal platform is fundamentally a persuasion problem rather than a technical one, and that it can be driven entirely by engineers without a product manager or executive mandate.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Reframes internal developer platform ROI as dependent on communication investment, not just tooling quality -- a signal for how platform engineering budgets and org design should be evaluated.
insights.quote: Technical adoption is a persuasion problem, not a technical one, and it can absolutely be driven by engineers.

insights.claim: The team had to sell their platform in two directions simultaneously: upward to management using business language (metrics, ROI) and across to developers by making operational pain tangible, with no product manager or executive sponsor to do either job for them.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: A reusable playbook for engineering-led platform teams operating without dedicated product/marketing support.
insights.quote: We had no product manager, no executive mandate. We also had a problem that a lot of platforms have; we had to sell in two directions.

insights.claim: Presenting DORA metrics gave the platform team a business-language seat at the table with management, turning a technical discussion into a business one, even though the numbers alone did not sustain the initiative.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Confirms DORA metrics remain a credible, board-legible proxy for engineering-productivity claims that platform and DevOps teams can use to secure buy-in.
insights.quote: DORA gave us a seat at the table. It turned a technical discussion into a business one.

insights.claim: An initial pilot measured a 77% faster deployment claim using DORA metrics, but the number didn't hold up under scrutiny because the measurement failed to capture hidden asynchronous processing in the full deployment chain, forcing the team to publicly own the error.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: A cautionary data point for any team citing DORA-style productivity metrics externally: measurement methodology gaps can undercut credibility once numbers are challenged, and the team pivoted away from leading with numbers alone.
insights.quote: We ran a pilot, measured 77% faster deployments, got buy-in -- and then the numbers didn't check out.

insights.claim: The team shifted developer messaging from a technical framing ("GitOps is technically better") to an emotional, persona-driven framing ("GitOps means I sleep through the night"), using relatable characters like Junior Dev Hans-Peter, On-call Olaf, and Feature-first Fiona to make hidden operational pain tangible, which measurably increased engagement and adoption.
insights.speaker: Ben Linders
insights.so_what: Demonstrates that narrative/persona techniques outperformed pure technical-pilot arguments for driving internal tool adoption -- a transferable tactic for any platform or infrastructure rollout facing developer resistance.
insights.quote: The shift wasn't "GitOps is technically better", it was "GitOps means I sleep through the night."
insights.locator: What happens when Hans-Peter deploys manually
